events.text: The patient is a 52-year-old female named Margaret Osei. She has a significant family history notable for her mother dying of ovarian cancer at age 58 and a maternal aunt with breast cancer diagnosed at age 44. She is a non-smoker, works as a high school biology teacher, is postmenopausal, and has two adult children. She has no prior cancer history.

At age 52 years, the patient had a diagnosis of FIGO Stage IIIC (AJCC 8th edition T3c N0 M0) HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA of the OVARY, BILATERAL (ICD-O-3 site code C56.9, histology code 8461/3). Relevant biomarkers: CA-125 at presentation 1,847 U/mL (reference < 35 U/mL); HE4 elevated at 412 pmol/L. Peritoneal carcinomatosis with omental caking confirmed. Ascites present (estimated 3.5 liters on imaging). Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant identified (see separate molecular report). Tumor grade: Grade 3 (high-grade). Laterality: Bilateral ovarian involvement. Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY via omental biopsy. Tumor Size: Right ovary 7.2 cm, Left ovary 5.8 cm. Lymph nodes: Negative for metastatic disease on surgical pathology. No distant metastases (liver surface implants present, no parenchymal involvement).

At age 52, the patient had a CT ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (staging), which showed cancer. Findings: Large bilateral ovarian masses (right 7.2 cm, left 5.8 cm) with internal complexity, thick septations, and solid nodular components. Extensive peritoneal carcinomatosis with omental caking measuring 14 x 9 cm in the greater omentum. Peritoneal implants scattered throughout the mesentery and bilateral paracolic gutters, largest implant 2.3 cm in the right hemidiaphragm. Moderate-to-large volume ascites. No liver parenchymal metastases; surface implants present on the hepatic dome. No pulmonary metastases on chest CT (separate study). No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy meeting size criteria. Impression: Findings consistent with bilateral ovarian primary malignancy with FIGO stage IIIC peritoneal dissemination. Surgical assessment for primary debulking recommended.

At age 52, the patient had a CT CHEST WITH CONTRAST (staging), which showed no cancer in the chest. Findings: No pulmonary nodules or masses. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. No pleural effusion. Small bilateral pleural thickening noted, likely reactive. Heart size normal. No osseous lesions. Impression: No evidence of intrathoracic metastatic disease. FIGO stage consistent with IIIC (not stage IV).

At age 52, the patient had a pathology result from DIAGNOSTIC LAPAROSCOPY / OMENTAL BIOPSY (pre-surgical tissue sampling for confirmation). Specimen: Omental biopsy. Gross description: Multiple tan-white firm nodules on fibrofatty omental tissue. Microscopic findings: Sheets and nests of highly atypical epithelial cells with high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio, prominent nucleoli, numerous mitotic figures (>10/hpf), and areas of necrosis. Psammoma bodies present. Immunohistochemistry: WT1 positive (diffuse), PAX8 positive, p53 aberrant (diffuse strong pattern, consistent with TP53 mutation), CK7 positive, CK20 negative, ER focally positive. Ki-67 proliferation index approximately 70%. Diagnosis: HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA, consistent with ovarian/tubal/peritoneal primary. Note: BRCA1/2 reflex germline testing ordered per institutional protocol.

At age 52, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma based on a specimen obtained from omental biopsy (peritoneum), which showed: Somatic alterations - TP53 p.R248W mutation (pathogenic, VAF 68%), homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) score 54 (positive, threshold >33); BRCA1 somatic allelic loss (loss of heterozygosity at BRCA1 locus, consistent with germline two-hit mechanism). Additionally: PIK3CA wild-type; KRAS wild-type; NRAS wild-type; CDK12 wild-type; CCNE1 copy number gain (low-level, 3 copies); MYC copy number gain (low-level); RB1 wild-type; PTEN wild-type; NF1 wild-type; ARID1A wild-type; BRCA2 wild-type (somatic). Tumor mutational burden (TMB): 5 mut/Mb (low). Microsatellite status: MSS (microsatellite stable). PD-L1 expression: CPS 2 (low). No NTRK fusions detected. No BRAF alterations. Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant confirmed on separate clinical germline testing: BRCA1 c.5266dupC (p.Gln1756ProfsTer74), classified as Pathogenic (ClinVar). HRD positive tumor with germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant - PARP inhibitor eligible.

At age 52, the patient had a gynecologic oncology consultation and multidisciplinary tumor board review. Presentation discussed in tumor board. Decision: Proceed with primary debulking surgery (PDS) given performance status ECOG 1, absence of significant comorbidities, and radiographic assessment suggesting resectability. Laparoscopic predictive index score 4 (favorable for complete cytoreduction). CA-125 1,847 U/mL. Patient and family counseled regarding prognosis, surgical goals (R0 resection target), and subsequent chemotherapy plan. Genetic counseling referral placed. Patient consented for surgery. Germline BRCA1 result communicated to patient - cascade testing recommended for first-degree relatives. Plan: TAH/BSO, omentectomy, peritoneal stripping, +/- bowel resection as needed. Target: no residual disease.

At age 52 years, the patient underwent PRIMARY DEBULKING SURGERY (PDS) via midline laparotomy. Procedures performed: Total abdominal hysterectomy (TAH), bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (BSO), infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, bilateral pelvic peritoneal stripping, right hemidiaphragm peritonectomy with diaphragmatic stripping (no full-thickness resection required), pelvic and para-aortic lymph node sampling, lysis of adhesions. Intraoperative findings: Extensive peritoneal carcinomatosis with omental caking; right ovary 7 cm hemorrhagic mass, left ovary 6 cm solid mass; diaphragmatic implants stripped completely; no bowel resection required. Estimated blood loss: 650 mL. Operative time: 5 hours 45 minutes. Postoperative residual disease: R0 (no visible residual disease, complete gross resection achieved). Surgeon impression: Optimal debulking with complete cytoreduction.

At age 52, the patient had a pathology result from PRIMARY DEBULKING SURGERY specimen (definitive surgical pathology). Specimens submitted: Right ovary/fallopian tube, left ovary/fallopian tube, uterus, omentum, appendix, pelvic peritoneum, diaphragm peritoneum, pelvic lymph nodes (16 nodes), para-aortic lymph nodes (11 nodes). Key findings: Right ovary: 7.2 cm high-grade serous carcinoma with capsular rupture and surface involvement. Left ovary: 5.8 cm high-grade serous carcinoma. Left fallopian tube: Serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC) in the fimbriated end - likely site of origin. Uterus: No myometrial invasion; cervix uninvolved. Omentum: Extensive involvement by high-grade serous carcinoma with peritoneal implants. Diaphragm peritoneum: Tumor implants present, all stripped (negative margins). Pelvic lymph nodes: 0/16 positive. Para-aortic lymph nodes: 0/11 positive. Appendix: No tumor involvement. Margins: All surgical margins negative. Final stage: pT3c pN0 pM0 (FIGO IIIC). TP53 aberrant pattern on IHC confirmed. BRCA1 protein loss by IHC (supportive of biallelic BRCA1 inactivation).

At age 52, the patient had a post-operative visit at 4 weeks following primary debulking surgery. Patient recovering well. No surgical complications. Incision healing well. She reports fatigue (grade 1), mild abdominal discomfort improving. Bowel function restored. No fever. Performance status ECOG 1. CA-125 post-operatively: 89 U/mL (significant decline from 1,847 U/mL, but not yet normalized). Plan: Begin adjuvant chemotherapy with carboplatin AUC 5 / paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 IV every 21 days for 6 cycles, with bevacizumab deferred given R0 resection and plan for PARP inhibitor maintenance. Baseline labs reviewed - CBC, CMP, and CA-125 obtained. Discussed PARP inhibitor maintenance after chemotherapy given germline BRCA1 and HRD-positive tumor. Patient agreeable to plan.

At age 52 years, the patient received curative-intent CARBOPLATIN AUC 5 / PACLITAXEL 175 mg/m2 IV every 21 days (Cycle 1 of 6) for high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, adjuvant setting post-R0 debulking. Premedications: dexamethasone, diphenhydramine, famotidine, ondansetron. Carboplatin dose calculated using Calvert formula (GFR estimated by CKD-EPI). Full doses administered without dose modification. Tolerated well. No hypersensitivity reaction. Cycle 1 initiated approximately 5 weeks post-operatively.

At age 52, the patient experienced a GRADE 2 PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY (sensory) following Cycle 3 of carboplatin/paclitaxel chemotherapy. Symptoms: tingling and numbness in bilateral fingertips and toes, affecting fine motor tasks (buttoning clothes). Graded per CTCAE v5.0 as Grade 2 (moderate symptoms, limiting instrumental ADLs). Management: Paclitaxel dose reduced to 135 mg/m2 (22% reduction) for Cycles 4-6. Carboplatin dose maintained at AUC 5. Patient counseled on neuropathy management including gabapentin (initiated at low dose), vitamin B6 avoidance, and activity modifications. Neurology referral not required at this time.

At age 52 years, the patient received CARBOPLATIN AUC 5 / PACLITAXEL 135 mg/m2 IV every 21 days (Cycles 4, 5, and 6 of 6) for high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, with paclitaxel dose-reduced from 175 to 135 mg/m2 due to Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy. All three cycles completed. Final cycle administered. CA-125 trending downward. Cycles completed without further dose modifications. Cumulative carboplatin dose delivered across 6 cycles achieved per plan.

At age 52-53, the patient had a CT ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (restaging after completion of 6 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel), which showed no cancer. Findings: No evidence of peritoneal carcinomatosis. No ascites. No suspicious pelvic or abdominal masses. Post-surgical changes consistent with prior debulking surgery (hysterectomy, BSO, omentectomy). No lymphadenopathy. No hepatic lesions. No pleural effusion. Impression: Complete radiographic response to primary debulking surgery and adjuvant chemotherapy. No evidence of residual or recurrent disease. CA-125 at time of imaging: 11 U/mL (normalized, reference < 35 U/mL).

At age 53, the patient had a gynecologic oncology post-chemotherapy assessment following completion of 6 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel. She has achieved a complete clinical response. CA-125 normalized to 11 U/mL. CT imaging shows no residual disease. She reports Grade 1 residual peripheral neuropathy (mild tingling in toes, not affecting daily activities). Fatigue improved significantly. Performance status ECOG 0-1. Discussed maintenance therapy options. Given germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant and HRD-positive tumor with complete response after platinum-based chemotherapy, niraparib maintenance is strongly indicated per PRIMA/ENGOT-OV26 data and SOLO1 data. Patient counseled extensively on niraparib: mechanism (PARP inhibitor), expected benefits (prolonged PFS), common side effects (thrombocytopenia, anemia, nausea, fatigue, hypertension), monitoring requirements (weekly CBC for first month, then monthly), and dose individualization strategy. Patient agreed to proceed with niraparib maintenance. Starting dose based on weight/platelet criteria: patient weight 68 kg, baseline platelets 187 x10^9/L - per individualized starting dose criteria, initiating at 200 mg daily (not 300 mg). Labs reviewed: Hgb 10.8 g/dL (mild anemia from chemotherapy), WBC 5.2 x10^9/L, platelets 187 x10^9/L.

At age 53 years, the patient received NIRAPARIB 200 mg orally once daily (maintenance therapy) initiated for high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma with germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant, following complete response to first-line carboplatin/paclitaxel. Individualized starting dose of 200 mg (based on weight < 77 kg and baseline platelet count < 150 x10^9/L criteria; platelet count borderline at 187 x10^9/L, provider elected individualized dosing). Supplied as 100 mg capsules, take 2 capsules once daily at bedtime to minimize nausea. Antiemetic prophylaxis prescribed (ondansetron PRN). Initiated approximately 6 weeks after last chemotherapy cycle.

At age 53, the patient experienced GRADE 3 THROMBOCYTOPENIA (platelet count < 50 x10^9/L) while on niraparib maintenance therapy. Timeline: Baseline platelets 187 x10^9/L prior to niraparib initiation; Week 2 CBC: 134 x10^9/L (Grade 1); Week 4 CBC: 62 x10^9/L (Grade 2); Week 6 CBC: 41 x10^9/L (Grade 3, platelet count < 50 x10^9/L). No bleeding symptoms. No petechiae or purpura on examination. Niraparib held per package insert guidance. Additional workup: peripheral smear reviewed (no schistocytes, no platelet clumping, findings consistent with drug-induced thrombocytopenia). HIT panel not indicated. Management: Niraparib held for 28 days. Platelet recovery monitored weekly. Repeat CBC at Day 14 of hold: platelets 98 x10^9/L (recovering). Repeat CBC at Day 28 of hold: platelets 156 x10^9/L (recovered to Grade 0). Plan: Resume niraparib at reduced dose of 100 mg daily per prescribing guidance for Grade 3 thrombocytopenia.

At age 53, the patient had an oncology visit for niraparib dose modification and re-initiation following Grade 3 thrombocytopenia. Platelet count has recovered to 156 x10^9/L after 28-day hold. No bleeding events during hold period. Patient reports fatigue (Grade 1) and mild nausea that were present on niraparib. She is tolerating the drug hold period well. Discussion: Resumed niraparib at 100 mg daily (dose reduction from 200 mg). Counseled that this is the expected lowest recommended dose and further dose reduction would require drug discontinuation. Monitoring plan: CBC weekly x 4 weeks, then every 4 weeks. CA-125 12 U/mL, remains normalized. Patient remains in complete remission. Peripheral neuropathy from prior paclitaxel: improving, now Grade 0-1.

At age 53 years, the patient resumed NIRAPARIB 100 mg orally once daily (dose-reduced maintenance therapy) following 28-day hold for Grade 3 thrombocytopenia. Dose reduced from 200 mg to 100 mg per prescribing guidance. Patient instructed to continue weekly CBC for 4 additional weeks, then monthly thereafter. Take at bedtime to minimize nausea. Continue ondansetron PRN for nausea. Patient agreeable and understands monitoring requirements.

At age 53, the patient experienced GRADE 1 NAUSEA and GRADE 1 FATIGUE on niraparib 100 mg daily (dose-reduced). These are manageable side effects. Nausea: mild, occurring primarily in the morning (despite taking medication at bedtime). Managed with ondansetron 4 mg PRN and dietary modifications (small frequent meals, avoidance of strong odors). Fatigue: mild, not limiting activities. No dose modification required. Platelet counts on 100 mg dose: Week 4 after resumption: 143 x10^9/L (Grade 1, acceptable), Week 8: 128 x10^9/L (Grade 1, stable), Week 12: 137 x10^9/L (Grade 1, stable). No further Grade 3+ thrombocytopenia. Monitoring continues monthly.

At age 53, the patient had a CT ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (surveillance, 6 months after completing chemotherapy / approximately 3 months after niraparib initiation), which showed no cancer. Findings: No peritoneal implants. No ascites. No pelvic masses. No lymphadenopathy. Post-surgical changes stable. No new lesions. Hepatic parenchyma normal. Impression: No evidence of disease recurrence. Complete radiographic remission maintained on niraparib maintenance therapy.

At age 53, the patient had a routine surveillance visit on niraparib maintenance therapy (approximately 6 months after chemotherapy completion). CA-125: 9 U/mL (normalized, stable). CT imaging confirms no evidence of disease. Tolerating niraparib 100 mg daily well with Grade 1 nausea and fatigue, managed conservatively. Platelet counts stable at Grade 1 range (128-143 x10^9/L) on monthly monitoring - no further dose reduction required. Peripheral neuropathy from prior paclitaxel: resolved (Grade 0). ECOG performance status: 0. Patient returned to full-time teaching. Genetic counseling completed - first-degree relatives counseled; daughter (age 26) pursuing genetic testing. Continue niraparib maintenance per plan (standard duration up to 3 years per SOLO1 data). Next surveillance CT in 6 months. Next CA-125 in 3 months.

At age 53-54, the patient had a CT ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (surveillance, 12 months after completing chemotherapy), which showed no cancer. Findings: No peritoneal carcinomatosis. No ascites. No pelvic or abdominal masses. No lymphadenopathy. No hepatic lesions. Stable post-surgical changes. Impression: Continued complete radiographic remission. No evidence of disease recurrence at 12-month post-chemotherapy surveillance.

At age 54, the patient had a 12-month post-chemotherapy surveillance visit. Patient doing well. CA-125: 8 U/mL (normalized, trending stable). CT imaging confirms ongoing complete remission. Niraparib 100 mg daily continued. Side effect profile: nausea Grade 0-1 (much improved, no longer requiring antiemetics regularly), fatigue Grade 0-1 (stable), thrombocytopenia Grade 1 (platelets 131 x10^9/L, stable). No further Grade 3+ hematologic toxicity since dose reduction. Quality of life: patient reports good quality of life, returned to normal activities. She mentions mild cognitive changes that may be related to prior chemotherapy ('chemo brain') but these are not significantly limiting. Menopause symptoms (hot flashes, vaginal dryness) managed with topical vaginal estrogen (local application acceptable per gynecologic oncology) and venlafaxine for hot flashes. Continue niraparib maintenance. Surveillance schedule: CT every 12 months, CA-125 every 3 months.

At age 54-55, the patient had a CT ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (surveillance, 24 months after completing chemotherapy), which showed no cancer. Findings: No evidence of peritoneal carcinomatosis. No ascites. No new masses. No lymphadenopathy. No hepatic lesions. Impression: Sustained complete radiographic remission at 24-month surveillance. No evidence of disease recurrence.

At age 55, the patient had a 24-month post-chemotherapy surveillance visit. Patient continues in complete remission. CA-125: 10 U/mL (stable, normalized). CT confirms no evidence of disease. Niraparib 100 mg daily continued - now 22 months on maintenance therapy. Tolerating well. CBC: platelets 138 x10^9/L (Grade 1, stable), Hgb 11.9 g/dL (mild anemia, Grade 1, stable), WBC 4.8 x10^9/L (normal). Peripheral neuropathy: fully resolved. Fatigue: Grade 0-1. Quality of life excellent. Patient's daughter (now 27) underwent BRCA1 genetic testing and was found to be a BRCA1 pathogenic variant carrier - referred to high-risk surveillance and risk-reduction counseling. Discussed plans for completing niraparib maintenance at 36 months per standard protocol. Continued long-term follow-up planned with CA-125 every 3 months and CT annually.

At age 55, the patient had a 36-month post-chemotherapy visit marking planned completion of niraparib maintenance therapy (approximately 36 months total duration per SOLO1 protocol). CA-125: 11 U/mL (stable). Patient remains in complete remission. Final niraparib dose taken. Overall assessment: Patient has achieved and maintained a complete sustained response to primary treatment (R0 debulking surgery + carboplatin/paclitaxel + niraparib maintenance) for FIGO IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma with germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant. Niraparib maintenance completed as planned. Transitioning to surveillance phase. Long-term surveillance plan: CA-125 every 3 months for years 4-5, then every 6 months thereafter; CT annually or symptom-directed; clinical examination every 6 months. Discussed signs/symptoms of potential recurrence. Patient counseled that risk of recurrence remains real even after 3-year remission, but prognosis is favorable with germline BRCA1 and complete initial response. Patient in good spirits, ECOG 0, fully active.

documents.text:
CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 52-year-old female with FIGO stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, status post primary debulking surgery (total abdominal hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, infracolic omentectomy, peritoneal stripping, right hemidiaphragm peritonectomy) followed by 6 cycles of adjuvant Paraplatin/Taxol chemotherapy, most recent cycle completed approximately 4 weeks prior. Restaging to assess treatment response. CA-125 at time of study: 11 U/mL.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed in the portal venous phase following administration of 100 mL of intravenous nonionic iodinated contrast (iohexol). Oral contrast administered. Axial acquisitions obtained with coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformations. Images reviewed on PACS workstation with bone and soft tissue algorithms. Reference comparison: Prior staging CT abdomen/pelvis performed at initial presentation.

FINDINGS:

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The hepatic parenchyma demonstrates normal attenuation and enhancement pattern. No focal hepatic lesions identified. Previously noted surface implants along the hepatic dome are no longer apparent. No perihepatic fluid. Portal vein and hepatic veins are patent.

Biliary system: No biliary ductal dilation. Gallbladder appears normal without stones or wall thickening.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesions.

Pancreas: The pancreatic parenchyma is homogeneous without ductal dilation, focal mass, or peripancreatic stranding.

Adrenal glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in morphology. No adenomas or suspicious lesions.

Kidneys: Both kidneys enhance symmetrically. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses or calculi. Normal excretion of contrast bilaterally.

Retroperitoneum and mesentery: The aorta and inferior vena cava are normal in caliber. No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymph nodes of pathologic size or character. Previously identified scattered mesenteric implants are no longer visualized. The mesentery appears clean with no nodularity, stranding, or infiltrative process.

Omentum: The greater omentum, which was extensively involved by tumor at presentation with a large omental cake (14 x 9 cm), is now surgically absent consistent with prior infracolic omentectomy. No residual omental nodularity or soft tissue density in the expected omental distribution.

Bowel: Small and large bowel loops are of normal caliber, distribution, and wall thickness throughout. No evidence of obstruction, wall thickening, or mucosal abnormality. Post-surgical changes noted in the region of the prior appendectomy site.

Peritoneum and paracolic gutters: No peritoneal soft tissue nodularity, plaque-like thickening, or implants identified along the peritoneal surfaces. The bilateral paracolic gutters are clear. No evidence of carcinomatosis.

ASCITES: No free intraperitoneal fluid. Complete resolution of the moderate-to-large volume ascites noted on the prior staging examination (estimated 3.5 liters at presentation).

DIAPHRAGM: The right hemidiaphragm is smooth in contour without nodularity or irregular thickening. Previously documented implant along the right hemidiaphragm has resolved. Surgical changes in the right subphrenic space are noted.

PELVIS:

Post-surgical anatomy: The uterus, ovaries, and fallopian tubes are surgically absent consistent with prior total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. The vaginal cuff appears intact without surrounding soft tissue density or mass. No hematoma or fluid collection at the pelvic surgical bed.

Urinary bladder: Normal wall thickness. No intraluminal mass. No perivesicular abnormality.

Pelvic floor and sidewalls: No pelvic sidewall masses or lymph nodes of pathologic character. No soft tissue nodularity along the pelvic peritoneal surfaces.

Bony pelvis: No suspicious osseous lesions.

LYMPH NODES: No pelvic, inguinal, retroperitoneal, or mesenteric lymph nodes meeting criteria for pathologic enlargement. No abnormal lymph node morphology.

BASE OF LUNG FIELDS: The visualized lung bases are clear bilaterally. No pleural effusion. No consolidation in the imaged lung bases. Previously noted small bilateral pleural thickening has resolved.

SKELETON: Visualized osseous structures including the lumbar spine, sacrum, and pelvis are without lytic or sclerotic lesion suspicious for metastatic involvement.

SOFT TISSUES: Midline laparotomy closure intact. Subcutaneous soft tissues without abnormality. Post-operative fluid collections or seromas not identified.

IMPRESSION:

1. Marked interval improvement from the prior staging examination. No peritoneal implants, omental disease, or carcinomatosis identified. Complete resolution of the previously extensive peritoneal tumor burden.

2. No ascites. Complete resolution of the large-volume ascites documented at initial presentation.

3. Hepatic dome surface implants previously noted at staging have resolved. No hepatic parenchymal or surface lesions.

4. Resolution of the right hemidiaphragm peritoneal implant following prior peritonectomy. Normal diaphragmatic contour.

5. No lymphadenopathy.

6. Post-surgical changes consistent with prior total abdominal hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, and peritoneal stripping procedures — all appear unremarkable without evidence of surgical complication.

7. No new or suspicious lesions identified in the abdomen or pelvis.

Overall, the imaging appearance is consistent with a complete radiographic response in the setting of primary debulking surgery followed by completion of adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy. This is concordant with the reported CA-125 normalization (11 U/mL at time of study).

Dictated by: [Attending Radiologist, Abdominal Imaging]
Electronically signed by: [Attending Radiologist]
Transcriptionist: [Staff]
Copies to: Gynecologic Oncology

GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Post-Chemotherapy Assessment / Maintenance Therapy Planning

ATTENDING: [Gynecologic Oncology Attending]
DEPARTMENT: Gynecologic Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Margaret Osei is a 53-year-old postmenopausal female with FIGO IIIC high-grade serous carcinoma of the ovary (bilateral), germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant carrier, presenting today for post-chemotherapy assessment following completion of her planned 6 cycles of adjuvant Paraplatin/PTX. She is here to discuss response evaluation and maintenance therapy planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Osei initially presented approximately 14 months ago with abdominal distension and early satiety. Workup revealed bilateral ovarian masses with extensive peritoneal carcinomatosis and ascites. CA-125 at presentation was markedly elevated at 1,847 U/mL. She underwent diagnostic laparoscopy with omental biopsy, which confirmed high-grade serous carcinoma. Germline testing identified a pathogenic BRCA1 variant (c.5266dupC, p.Gln1756ProfsTer74). NGS additionally demonstrated homologous recombination deficiency (HRD score 54, positive) and TP53 p.R248W somatic mutation.

She was presented at multidisciplinary tumor board and proceeded with primary debulking surgery (TAH/BSO, infracolic omentectomy, bilateral pelvic peritoneal stripping, right hemidiaphragm peritonectomy, appendectomy, pelvic and para-aortic lymph node sampling) achieving an R0 resection — no visible residual disease. Final surgical pathology confirmed pT3c pN0 pM0, FIGO IIIC, with the likely site of origin being serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC) identified at the fimbriated end of the left fallopian tube. All 27 sampled lymph nodes were negative.

Following recovery, she began adjuvant chemotherapy with Paraplatin AUC 5 / PTX 175 mg/m2 IV q21 days. During Cycle 3, she developed Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy (sensory) with bilateral distal fingertip and toe tingling affecting fine motor tasks. PTX was dose-reduced to 135 mg/m2 for Cycles 4 through 6, with Paraplatin maintained at AUC 5. She completed all 6 cycles without further interruption. CA-125 has been trending down appropriately throughout treatment.

Restaging CT abdomen/pelvis performed after Cycle 6 showed no evidence of peritoneal carcinomatosis, no ascites, no suspicious masses or lymphadenopathy — complete radiographic response. CA-125 today returns at 11 U/mL (normalized; reference < 35 U/mL). She is presenting today for assessment of her response and to discuss transition to maintenance therapy.

She currently reports Grade 1 residual sensory neuropathy — mild tingling in the toes only, not involving fingers, not affecting any daily activities. She notes her fatigue has substantially improved since completing chemotherapy and she is back to most of her normal activities. Appetite is good. No nausea or vomiting currently. No abdominal pain, bloating, or new pelvic symptoms. No dyspnea. No lower extremity edema.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Fatigue improving, Grade 1 at most. No fevers, chills, or night sweats.
- Neurologic: Residual distal toe tingling, Grade 1. No new neurologic symptoms.
- GI: Appetite good. Normal bowel habits. No nausea at present. No abdominal pain.
- GU: No urinary symptoms.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no lower extremity swelling.
- Musculoskeletal: Mild myalgias occasionally, improving.
- Hematologic: No bruising, no unusual bleeding.
- Endocrine/Gynecologic: Postmenopausal. Experiencing hot flashes (moderate), vaginal dryness. No abnormal bleeding (status post hysterectomy).
- Psychiatric: Mood good. Some anxiety about maintenance therapy and potential side effects, addressed during visit.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm, temperature 37.0 C, weight 68 kg, height 165 cm, BMI 25.0.
Performance status: ECOG 0-1.

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented. Engaged in conversation, asking appropriate and informed questions.
HEENT: No scleral icterus. Mucous membranes moist.
Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpated.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles or wheezing.
Abdomen: Well-healed midline laparotomy scar. Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No palpable masses. No hepatosplenomegaly. No ascites appreciated on exam.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. No calf tenderness.
Skin: No petechiae, ecchymoses, or rash.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves intact. Strength 5/5 throughout. Light touch sensation mildly decreased in bilateral toes only. Reflexes symmetric.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:
CA-125: 11 U/mL (reference < 35 U/mL) — normalized
CBC:
  - WBC: 5.2 x10^9/L (normal)
  - Hemoglobin: 10.8 g/dL (mild anemia, Grade 1 by CTCAE — expected post-chemotherapy)
  - Platelets: 187 x10^9/L (low-normal range)
  - ANC: 3.1 x10^9/L (normal)
Comprehensive metabolic panel:
  - Creatinine: 0.82 mg/dL, eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2
  - LFTs: within normal limits (ALT 22, AST 19, ALP 68, total bilirubin 0.7)
  - Electrolytes: within normal limits
Thyroid function (TSH): 2.1 mIU/L (normal)

---

IMAGING:
Restaging CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (performed after completion of Cycle 6): No evidence of peritoneal carcinomatosis. No ascites. No suspicious pelvic or abdominal masses. No lymphadenopathy. No hepatic lesions. Post-surgical changes consistent with prior debulking surgery (hysterectomy, BSO, omentectomy), stable. Impression: Complete radiographic response to surgery and adjuvant chemotherapy.

---

ASSESSMENT:
1. FIGO IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, bilateral — COMPLETE CLINICAL AND RADIOGRAPHIC RESPONSE following primary debulking surgery (R0) and 6 cycles of adjuvant carbo/PTX. CA-125 normalized to 11 U/mL. CT with no evidence of residual or recurrent disease.

2. Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC), HRD-positive tumor — PARP inhibitor maintenance strongly indicated.

3. Grade 1 residual chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (CIPN) — mild, distal toes only, improving.

4. Grade 1 anemia (Hgb 10.8 g/dL) — expected post-chemotherapy bone marrow recovery; not requiring intervention at this time.

5. Menopausal symptoms (surgical/natural menopause) — managed symptomatically.

---

PLAN:

1. MAINTENANCE THERAPY — Zejula (Zejula):
Ms. Osei has achieved a complete response to platinum-based first-line therapy and carries a germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant with an HRD-positive tumor. She is an ideal candidate for PARP inhibitor maintenance per the SOLO-1 data (Lynparza) and the PRIMA/ENGOT-OV26 data (Zejula), both of which demonstrate highly significant progression-free survival benefit in this population. I discussed both nira and ola; after shared decision-making, we will proceed with Zejula.

Dosing rationale: Per individualized starting dose guidance, initiating at 200 mg daily rather than 300 mg. Grade 1, resolving. No active intervention required. Continue monitoring. Will reassess at next visit.

Hgb 10.8 g/dL, Grade 1, asymptomatic. Expected to recover with time off cytotoxic chemotherapy. No erythropoietin-stimulating agent indicated at this time. Will monitor with monthly CBC on Zejula.

Discussed management options. CA-125 every 3 months while on maintenance therapy. CT abdomen/pelvis in approximately 6 months (surveillance imaging). Clinical exam at each visit.

Genetic counseling was completed. Cascade testing for first-degree relatives was recommended at prior visit. Patient's daughter is pursuing genetic testing. Patient counseled to encourage her son and any maternal relatives to consider testing as well.

Return to clinic in 4 weeks for CBC review and tolerance assessment on Zejula. Will adjust dose as needed based on laboratory and clinical findings.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 52-year-old female presenting with progressive abdominal distension, pelvic pressure, and elevated CA-125 (1,847 U/mL). Pelvic ultrasound demonstrated bilateral complex adnexal masses. CT requested for staging evaluation.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the abdomen and pelvis performed following administration of 120 mL of intravenous iohexol contrast agent in portal venous phase. Axial images acquired at 2.5 mm slice thickness with multiplanar reformations in coronal and sagittal planes. No oral contrast administered given acute presentation. Images reviewed on PACS workstation.

---

FINDINGS:

PELVIS / ADNEXA:
There are large bilateral adnexal masses. The right adnexal mass measures approximately 7.2 x 6.1 x 5.8 cm and demonstrates marked internal complexity with thick irregular septations, solid enhancing mural nodules, and areas of mixed attenuation suggesting hemorrhage and/or necrosis. The left adnexal mass measures approximately 5.8 x 4.9 x 4.5 cm with similar morphology — thick septations, lobulated contour, and avidly enhancing solid components. Both lesions are intimately associated with the respective ovaries; the uterus is displaced superiorly but appears grossly intact without obvious myometrial involvement. The cul-de-sac is partially effaced by dependent peritoneal studding. Normal fat planes around the bladder are partially obscured by peritoneal disease; no frank bladder wall invasion is identified.

PERITONEAL CAVITY / OMENTUM:
Extensive peritoneal carcinomatosis is present throughout the abdomen and pelvis. The greater omentum is largely replaced by confluent soft tissue — a large irregular "cake" of tumor measuring approximately 14 x 9 cm in greatest axial dimensions occupies the infracolic omental compartment, abutting the transverse colon without clear evidence of intramural colonic invasion. Scattered discrete peritoneal implants are seen along the right and left paracolic gutters, the pelvic sidewalls, and draped over the sigmoid mesentery and small bowel serosa. The largest discrete implant identified is located at the right hemidiaphragm, measuring approximately 2.3 cm, with pleural surface abutment but no apparent extension into the pulmonary parenchyma. Additional sub-centimeter implants are present along the left hemidiaphragm. The small bowel loops are displaced centrally by the omental mass but no bowel obstruction is evident; the mesenteric root shows soft tissue infiltration and tethering of loops consistent with peritoneal seeding.

ASCITES:
Moderate-to-large volume free intraperitoneal fluid is present throughout the pelvis, paracolic gutters, and perihepatic/perisplenic spaces, estimated at approximately 3.5 liters. The fluid is simple in attenuation without internal septations or complexity.

LIVER AND BILIARY SYSTEM:
The hepatic parenchyma enhances homogeneously without focal hypoattenuating or hyperattenuating lesions to suggest parenchymal metastatic involvement. Several small peritoneal implants are seen plastered against the hepatic dome surface (capsular), most prominently a 1.1 cm soft tissue nodule along the superior dome of the right hepatic lobe. No intrahepatic biliary dilation. Gallbladder is unremarkable. No choledocholithiasis.

SPLEEN:
Spleen is mildly enlarged at 13.2 cm in craniocaudal dimension, likely related to ascites and underlying disease burden. No focal splenic lesions.

PANCREAS:
Pancreas is normal in contour, attenuation, and ductal caliber. No peripancreatic changes.

KIDNEYS AND ADRENALS:
Bilateral kidneys are normal in size and enhancement without hydronephrosis, stones, or focal lesions. Adrenal glands are unremarkable bilaterally.

GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT:
Stomach appears normal. Colon is decompressed without obstruction. As noted, the transverse colon is closely abutted by omental tumor cake superiorly but no definitive intramural invasion is seen. Appendix is visualized and appears within normal limits.

RETROPERITONEUM AND LYMPH NODES:
No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymph nodes that meet size criteria for enlargement. The aorta and inferior vena cava are patent without displacement or encasement. No definite iliac chain adenopathy.

VASCULAR:
Aorta is normal in caliber. No aneurysmal change. Portal and hepatic veins are patent.

BONY STRUCTURES AND SOFT TISSUES:
Visible thoracolumbar vertebral bodies are intact without lytic or blastic lesions. Pelvic bony structures appear preserved. Abdominal wall and soft tissues are unremarkable aside from the ascites-related distension.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Large bilateral complex adnexal masses — right 7.2 cm, left 5.8 cm — morphologically highly concerning for primary ovarian malignancy. The internal architecture (thick septations, solid enhancing nodules, mixed attenuation) is in keeping with high-grade serous carcinoma, though tissue sampling is required for definitive histologic diagnosis.

2. Extensive peritoneal carcinomatosis including a dominant omental cake (approximately 14 x 9 cm) in the infracolic compartment, scattered mesenteric and paracolic gutter implants, and pelvic peritoneal seeding throughout the cul-de-sac and pelvic sidewalls.

3. Peritoneal implants along the right hemidiaphragm (up to 2.3 cm) and left hemidiaphragm; no frank hepatic parenchymal lesions, though capsular surface deposits on the hepatic dome are present. 4. Moderate-to-large volume ascites.

5. No convincing retroperitoneal or pelvic adenopathy by size criteria.

6. No bowel obstruction identified at this time.

These findings are highly suspicious for advanced bilateral ovarian primary malignancy with diffuse peritoneal spread. Correlation with serum tumor markers and tissue sampling is recommended. The degree of peritoneal disease and diaphragmatic involvement should be discussed with the surgical team regarding feasibility of upfront cytoreductive surgery versus neoadjuvant chemotherapy approach.

---

GROSS DESCRIPTION:
Received fresh, labeled "omental biopsy," are multiple irregular fragments of fibrofatty soft tissue in aggregate measuring 4.2 x 3.1 x 1.0 cm. The tissue is tan-yellow with lobulated adipose admixed with firm, tan-white, gritty nodular deposits ranging from 0.3 to 1.8 cm in greatest dimension. The nodules have a hard, chalky consistency on sectioning. No grossly hemorrhagic or cystic areas identified within the submitted fragments. Representative sections entirely submitted in two cassettes (A1-A2).

---

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:
Histologic sections reveal fibrofatty omental tissue extensively infiltrated by a malignant epithelial neoplasm. The tumor is arranged in solid sheets, papillary clusters, and irregular nests and glands with slit-like spaces. The neoplastic cells are large and polygonal with markedly enlarged, pleomorphic nuclei, coarse chromatin, and conspicuous macronucleoli. The nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio is markedly elevated. Mitotic figures are numerous, exceeding 10 per 10 high-power fields, including atypical mitoses. Extensive areas of geographic tumor necrosis are present. Scattered concentrically laminated calcifications (psammoma bodies) are identified within several papillary clusters, a finding highly characteristic of serous differentiation.

The background stroma shows a desmoplastic reaction with scattered chronic inflammatory infiltrate. No lymphovascular invasion is definitively identified in this biopsy specimen. No normal omental tissue is seen within the submitted tumor-bearing fragments.

DIAGNOSIS:

A. OMENTUM, BIOPSY (LAPAROSCOPIC):
   - HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA
   - Immunohistochemical profile (WT1+, PAX8+, CK7+, CK20-, p53 aberrant, ER focal+) is consistent with an ovarian, fallopian tube, or peritoneal primary
   - Ki-67 proliferation index markedly elevated (~70%)
   - Psammoma bodies present
   - Aberrant p53 expression pattern (diffuse strong overexpression) consistent with underlying TP53 mutation

---

COMMENT:
The morphologic features and immunophenotype are diagnostic of high-grade serous carcinoma. The combination of diffuse WT1 and PAX8 positivity, aberrant p53, high Ki-67, and the presence of psammoma bodies strongly supports Müllerian (ovarian/tubo-peritoneal) origin. CK7-positive, CK20-negative, Napsin A-negative, and TTF-1-negative staining effectively excludes metastatic lung adenocarcinoma and colorectal primary. Absence of GATA3 staining argues against breast primary. Clinical and radiographic correlation with bilateral ovarian masses and peritoneal dissemination is consistent with a primary gynecologic high-grade serous carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC.

Germline BRCA1/2 testing has been reflexed per institutional gynecologic oncology protocol given the diagnosis of high-grade serous carcinoma in a woman with relevant family history.

INTERPRETATION
========================================================
This high-grade serous carcinoma of ovarian/peritoneal/tubal origin demonstrates a genomic profile highly characteristic of the HGSOC molecular subtype:

1. TP53 p.R248W (VAF 68%): A gain-of-function hotspot mutation in TP53 is present at high allele frequency, consistent with clonal, biallelic TP53 inactivation (the second hit likely being the wild-type allele loss at 17p, consistent with the elevated HRD score). TP53 mutation is near-universal in HGSOC (~96%) and is consistent with the aberrant (diffuse strong) p53 immunohistochemical pattern observed on the diagnostic biopsy. This variant is not directly actionable therapeutically but confirms the diagnosis.

2. Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant + somatic LOH (biallelic inactivation): The tumor has undergone biallelic BRCA1 inactivation via the two-hit mechanism: germline frameshift variant (first hit) plus somatic loss of heterozygosity at the BRCA1 locus (second hit). Functional BRCA1 protein is absent in tumor cells, resulting in defective homologous recombination DNA repair. This is the primary driver of HRD positivity and is the most clinically actionable finding in this report.

3. HRD Score 54 (positive): Consistent with an HR-deficient phenotype. This finding, in combination with platinum-based chemotherapy sensitivity, supports eligibility for PARP inhibitor-based maintenance therapy.

4. Low-level CCNE1 and MYC copy number gains: These low-level copy number gains are of uncertain clinical significance. CCNE1 amplification (high-level) is a known mechanism of PARP inhibitor resistance in HGSOC, but low-level gain at 3 copies does not meet the threshold for amplification and its functional significance is unclear at this time. CCNE1 status should be re-evaluated if platinum resistance develops in the future.

5. TMB low, MSS, PD-L1 low: These findings are typical for HGSOC. Standard checkpoint inhibitor indications (TMB-high, MSI-H/dMMR) are not met.

    - Niraparib (Zejula): FDA-approved as maintenance therapy for adult patients with advanced epithelial ovarian, fallopian tube, or primary peritoneal cancer who are in complete or partial response to first-line platinum-based chemotherapy, regardless of BRCA or HRD status (PRIMA/ENGOT-OV26 basis; HRD-positive subgroup showed greatest benefit)
    - Rucaparib (Rubraca): FDA-approved for BRCA-mutated advanced ovarian cancer (recurrent setting after 2+ prior chemotherapy lines)
  Clinical Note: Patient is HRD-positive with germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant. CHIEF COMPLAINT
Ms. Margaret Osei is a 52-year-old postmenopausal woman referred for evaluation and management of newly diagnosed bilateral ovarian masses with peritoneal carcinomatosis and ascites, confirmed high-grade serous carcinoma on omental biopsy. She presents today for gynecologic oncology consultation, tumor board discussion, and surgical planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Osei is a 52-year-old postmenopausal female with no prior malignancy who presented to her primary care physician several weeks ago with progressive abdominal distension, early satiety, and a 10-pound unintentional weight loss over approximately 2 months. She was noted to have significant abdominal ascites on physical examination and was referred for expedited workup. CA-125 was markedly elevated at 1,847 U/mL (reference < 35 U/mL). HE4 was also elevated at 412 pmol/L.

CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast demonstrated large bilateral ovarian masses (right 7.2 cm, left 5.8 cm) with internal complexity, thick septations, and solid nodular components. Extensive peritoneal carcinomatosis was identified, with omental caking measuring 14 x 9 cm in the greater omentum. Peritoneal implants were noted throughout the mesentery and bilateral paracolic gutters, with the largest implant measuring 2.3 cm at the right hemidiaphragm. Estimated ascites volume was 3.5 liters. No liver parenchymal lesions were identified, though surface implants were present at the hepatic dome. CT chest was negative for intrathoracic disease. These findings are consistent with FIGO stage IIIC bilateral ovarian primary malignancy.

Diagnostic laparoscopy with omental biopsy was subsequently performed for tissue confirmation. Final pathology demonstrated high-grade serous carcinoma with diffuse WT1 and PAX8 positivity, aberrant p53 pattern (diffuse strong staining, consistent with TP53 mutation), and a Ki-67 proliferation index of approximately 70%. The histology is consistent with ovarian/tubal/peritoneal primary.

Next-generation sequencing (NGS) on the omental biopsy specimen identified a somatic TP53 p.R248W mutation (VAF 68%), homologous recombination deficiency (HRD score 54, above the positive threshold of 33), and biallelic BRCA1 inactivation via somatic loss of heterozygosity at the BRCA1 locus. Germline testing (sent per institutional protocol at time of biopsy) has returned and confirms a pathogenic BRCA1 germline variant: BRCA1 c.5266dupC (p.Gln1756ProfsTer74), classified as Pathogenic in ClinVar. Tumor mutational burden was low at 5 mut/Mb. Microsatellite status is stable. The tumor is HRD-positive with a germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant, indicating eligibility for PARP inhibitor therapy.

This case was discussed today at the weekly Gynecologic Oncology Multidisciplinary Tumor Board attended by gynecologic oncology, medical oncology, radiology, and pathology. A summary of the discussion and plan is detailed below.

ASSESSMENT
Ms. Osei is a 52-year-old postmenopausal woman with newly diagnosed FIGO Stage IIIC (T3c N0 M0 by AJCC 8th edition) bilateral high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma with:
- Extensive peritoneal carcinomatosis and omental caking
- Ascites (~3.5 liters)
- Markedly elevated CA-125 (1,847 U/mL)
- Confirmed germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC)
- HRD-positive tumor (score 54)
- ECOG performance status 1
- No significant comorbidities
- Favorable LPI score (4) suggesting resectability

This case was reviewed at the Multidisciplinary Tumor Board. All attendees reviewed CT imaging, pathology, and NGS findings. Consensus recommendation:

Proceed with PRIMARY DEBULKING SURGERY (PDS). Rationale: ECOG PS 1, no significant comorbidities, LPI score 4 indicating favorable resectability, imaging suggesting disease predominantly within the abdomen/pelvis without bulky retroperitoneal nodes, and patient's strong preference to pursue aggressive surgical management after informed discussion. The goal is R0 resection (no visible residual disease), which is the most important prognostic factor in advanced ovarian cancer.

---

PLAN

1. PRIMARY DEBULKING SURGERY (PDS):
   - Planned procedure: Total abdominal hysterectomy (TAH), bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (BSO), infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, peritoneal stripping, right hemidiaphragm peritonectomy as needed, pelvic and para-aortic lymph node sampling.
   - Goal: R0 resection (no visible residual disease). Bowel resection will be performed if required for complete cytoreduction, per intraoperative findings.
   - Approach: Midline laparotomy.
   - Anesthesia and surgical team pre-operative assessment initiated.
   - Patient counseled extensively about goals of surgery, risks (bleeding, infection, bowel injury, need for colostomy if bowel resection required, DVT/PE risk, prolonged recovery), and the critical importance of achieving complete gross resection for optimal outcomes.
   - Patient expressed understanding and consent obtained.

2. ADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY (post-surgical plan):
   - Following R0 resection, planned adjuvant chemotherapy with carboplatin AUC 5 / paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 IV every 21 days for 6 cycles.
   - Bevacizumab addition discussed; deferred given plan for PARP inhibitor maintenance and in light of R0 resection goal.
   - Intraperitoneal chemotherapy discussed (HIPEC approach); not currently planned at this institution per standard of care.

3. MAINTENANCE THERAPY:
   - Given germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant and HRD-positive tumor, patient will be strongly considered for PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy (niraparib or olaparib per SOLO1 data) following completion of platinum-based chemotherapy if complete/partial response achieved.
   - This will be finalized after post-chemotherapy response assessment.

4. GERMLINE BRCA1 COUNSELING:
   - Germline BRCA1 c.5266dupC result communicated to patient and husband today in detail.
   - Implications explained: hereditary breast and ovarian cancer (HBOC) syndrome; implications for first-degree relatives (children and siblings carry 50% risk of inheriting pathogenic variant).
   - Cascade genetic testing strongly recommended for her two adult children and any siblings.
   - Formal genetic counseling referral placed today. Patient agreeable.
   - BRCA1 pathogenic variant also has implications for treatment (PARP inhibitor eligibility) -- reviewed above.

5. ANCILLARY MEASURES:
   - Nutritional support: Referral to oncology dietitian given mild hypoalbuminemia and nutritional deficiencies in context of malignancy.
   - DVT prophylaxis: Low molecular weight heparin (LMWH) prophylaxis to be initiated per perioperative protocol.
   - Pre-operative bowel prep as per surgical team preference.
   - Pre-operative CBC/CMP/coagulation to be repeated within 1 week of surgery.

6. FOLLOW-UP:
   - Patient to return for pre-operative visit with surgical team prior to PDS.
   - Post-operative gynecologic oncology visit approximately 3-4 weeks after surgery to assess recovery and initiate chemotherapy planning.

Total time with patient and family: approximately 75 minutes. All questions addressed. Patient and husband in understanding and agreement with plan. Patient is emotionally distraught but engaged and determined.

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION AND DIAGNOSTIC FINDINGS

Specimen A — Right Ovary and Fallopian Tube:
Sections of the right ovary show near-total replacement of the ovarian parenchyma by a high-grade epithelial malignancy. The tumor displays architectural patterns characteristic of high-grade serous carcinoma: solid sheets, nests, papillary structures, and slit-like spaces lined by markedly atypical epithelial cells. The neoplastic cells are large with high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios, pleomorphic vesicular nuclei, macronucleoli, and brisk mitotic activity (readily exceeding 10 mitotic figures per high-power field). Atypical mitotic figures are identified. Areas of geographic necrosis are present. Psammoma bodies are focally present. Lymphovascular space invasion (LVSI) is identified. The tumor extends through the ovarian capsule with surface involvement confirmed microscopically (capsular rupture present). Sections of the right fallopian tube show a benign epithelial lining with no evidence of serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC) or invasive carcinoma.

Diagnosis — Specimen A: HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA extensively involving the right ovary, with capsular rupture and surface involvement. Right fallopian tube: no malignancy identified.

Specimen B — Left Ovary and Fallopian Tube:
Sections of the left ovary show replacement by the same high-grade serous carcinoma as described in Specimen A, with solid and papillary growth patterns, marked nuclear pleomorphism, brisk mitosis, and focal necrosis. Diagnosis — Specimen B: HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA extensively involving the left ovary with surface involvement; SEROUS TUBAL INTRAEPITHELIAL CARCINOMA (STIC) of the left fallopian tube fimbriated end.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Osei underwent primary debulking surgery (PDS) via midline laparotomy approximately 4 weeks ago. Procedures performed included TAH/BSO, infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, bilateral pelvic peritoneal stripping, right hemidiaphragm peritonectomy with diaphragmatic stripping, and bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node sampling. Intraoperative and postoperative course was notable for an estimated blood loss of 650 mL with no intraoperative complications; no bowel resection was required. She was discharged from the hospital on postoperative day 5 in good condition.

At today's visit, the patient reports that her recovery has been progressing well overall. She notes persistent fatigue, which she describes as moderate -- she is able to perform basic activities of daily living independently but has not yet returned to work. She reports mild residual abdominal soreness around the midline incision, which is improving week over week. Bowel function has fully restored; she is tolerating a regular diet without difficulty. No nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. No urinary symptoms. She denies fever, chills, wound drainage, or erythema. She has not had any signs of wound dehiscence.

Of note, surgical pathology from the debulking specimen has confirmed FIGO pT3c pN0 pM0 high-grade serous carcinoma with: bilateral ovarian involvement (right 7.2 cm, left 5.8 cm), serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC) in the left fimbriated end (likely site of origin), extensive omental involvement, diaphragmatic peritoneal implants (all stripped with negative margins), negative pelvic lymph nodes (0/16) and para-aortic lymph nodes (0/11), and negative uterine and cervical involvement. All surgical margins were negative. Optimal cytoreduction was achieved with no visible residual disease (R0).

CA-125, drawn approximately 2 weeks post-operatively, has declined markedly from a pre-operative value of 1,847 U/mL to 89 U/mL. This is encouraging and consistent with successful gross cytoreduction, though the value has not yet normalized to the laboratory reference range of <35 U/mL. Serial monitoring will continue.

The patient and her family have been informed of the surgical and pathological results, including the favorable cytoreduction outcome. She has been counseled regarding the planned adjuvant chemotherapy regimen and the subsequent maintenance strategy given her germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant and HRD-positive tumor profile. She expressed understanding and is ready to move forward with treatment.

---

- Constitutional: Fatigue Grade 1 (improving). No fevers. No night sweats. No significant unintentional weight loss since surgery.
- GI: Appetite returning. No nausea or emesis. Bowel function normal. No abdominal distension or bloating. No constipation. No blood in stool.
- GU: No urinary frequency or urgency beyond baseline. No hematuria. No dysuria.
- Neurologic: No numbness, tingling, or weakness. No headache.
- Musculoskeletal: Mild generalized deconditioning from surgery. No joint pain.
- Skin: No rash. Incision healing without complication.
- Psychiatric: Reports some anxiety about starting chemotherapy; denies depression.
- All other systems reviewed and negative.

1. High-grade serous carcinoma of the ovary, bilateral, FIGO IIIC (T3c N0 M0), germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (BRCA1 c.5266dupC, p.Gln1756ProfsTer74) - diagnosed this year
2. Postmenopausal status (natural menopause)
3. No prior malignancy
4. No prior abdominal/pelvic surgery prior to current diagnosis

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:
- Primary debulking surgery (TAH/BSO, infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, bilateral pelvic peritoneal stripping, right diaphragmatic peritonectomy, pelvic and para-aortic lymph node sampling) - 4 weeks prior to this visit
- Diagnostic laparoscopy with omental biopsy for tissue diagnosis - prior to PDS

FAMILY HISTORY:

- Maternal aunt: Breast cancer diagnosed at age 44

- General: Well-appearing woman, alert and oriented x3, no acute distress. Slightly fatigued-appearing but in good spirits.
- HEENT: Anicteric sclerae. Mucous membranes moist. No lymphadenopathy.
- Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops.

- Abdomen: Midline vertical incision well-approximated, healing appropriately, no erythema, no purulent discharge, no induration. Steri-strips in place over lower portion. Abdomen soft. Mild tenderness to deep palpation at the surgical site, appropriate for healing. No guarding or rigidity. No palpable masses. Bowel sounds present in all quadrants. No ascites appreciated on exam.
- Extremities: No lower extremity edema. Peripheral pulses intact. No calf tenderness.
- Neurologic: Alert, oriented. Cranial nerves grossly intact. No focal deficits. Sensation intact to light touch in bilateral upper and lower extremities.
- Performance status: ECOG 1 (restricted in physically strenuous activity but ambulatory, able to carry out light work)

- CBC: WBC 6.4 x10^9/L (normal), Hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL (low, Grade 1 anemia - expected post-operatively), Hematocrit 29.3%, Platelets 224 x10^9/L (normal)
- CMP: Na 138, K 4.0, Cl 102, CO2 24, BUN 12, Creatinine 0.82 mg/dL (eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2), Glucose 91. LFTs: AST 22, ALT 18, Alk Phos 74, Total Bilirubin 0.6. Albumin 3.6.

IMAGING / PATHOLOGY REVIEW:
Surgical pathology from primary debulking surgery reviewed and discussed with patient. Final diagnosis: high-grade serous carcinoma, bilateral ovaries (right 7.2 cm, left 5.8 cm), with STIC in left fallopian tube, extensive omental and peritoneal involvement, diaphragmatic implants (completely resected, negative margins). pT3c pN0 pM0. Lymph nodes: 0/16 pelvic, 0/11 para-aortic negative. All surgical margins negative. R0 resection confirmed. No residual gross disease. TP53 aberrant by IHC. BRCA1 protein loss by IHC (biallelic inactivation). No pre-operative CT needed at this time; post-operative baseline imaging will be performed after completion of chemotherapy.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Osei is a 52-year-old woman with FIGO IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, bilateral, who has achieved R0 cytoreduction at primary debulking surgery. She is now 4 weeks post-operatively with excellent wound healing, improving performance status (ECOG 1), and a markedly declined but not yet normalized CA-125 of 89 U/mL. She is otherwise clinically well and ready to begin adjuvant systemic chemotherapy.

1. ADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY - INITIATION:
   Standard of care adjuvant platinum-taxane chemotherapy will be initiated. Plan: Paraplatin AUC 5 / PTX 175 mg/m2 IV every 21 days for 6 cycles. Paraplatin dose will be calculated using the Calvert formula with GFR estimated by CKD-EPI (current creatinine 0.82 mg/dL, estimated GFR ~85 mL/min). PTX dose based on BSA (patient weight 68 kg, height to be confirmed at infusion visit). Standard premedications will be administered: dexamethasone 20 mg IV, diphenhydramine 25-50 mg IV, famotidine 20 mg IV, and ondansetron 8 mg IV. Cycle 1 scheduled for approximately 5 weeks post-operatively to allow adequate recovery. Full blood counts and chemistry confirmed acceptable for initiation. Patient counseled on anticipated side effects including alopecia (near-universal with this regimen), nausea, fatigue, myelosuppression, and peripheral neuropathy (cumulative with PTX). She will be given a cold cap consultation at patient's request and has been advised of partial efficacy. Cooling gloves/socks will be discussed to potentially mitigate neuropathy. G-CSF prophylaxis: not routinely indicated for this regimen; will administer if significant neutropenia develops.

   Avastin deferred at this time: given achievement of R0 resection and the plan for PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy post-chemotherapy, bev is not being added to the adjuvant regimen. GOG-0218 data supports Avastin in a subset of patients, but PARP inhibitor maintenance is prioritized for this germline BRCA1 carrier.

2. PARP INHIBITOR MAINTENANCE THERAPY - PLANNING:
   Following completion of 6 cycles of adjuvant Paraplatin/PTX, and assuming disease response, the patient will proceed to nira maintenance therapy. This is strongly supported by SOLO1 data demonstrating significantly prolonged progression-free survival in germline BRCA1/2 carriers with advanced ovarian cancer who achieve complete or partial response to first-line platinum-based chemotherapy. Her HRD-positive tumor further supports this. Will discuss specific dosing (individualized starting dose strategy based on weight and platelet parameters) closer to chemotherapy completion. Patient expressed understanding and enthusiasm for this plan.

3. ANEMIA:
   Post-operative anemia with Hgb 9.8 g/dL, Grade 1. Continue iron supplementation. Recheck CBC prior to Cycle 1. If Hgb <8.0 g/dL or symptomatic, consider erythropoiesis-stimulating agent or transfusion per institutional guidelines. Expected to improve with continued iron repletion and post-operative recovery. ESA use will be guided by anticipated chemotherapy-related anemia as well.

4. CA-125 MONITORING:
   CA-125 is 89 U/mL, substantially reduced from pre-operative 1,847 U/mL. This trajectory is encouraging and consistent with successful cytoreduction. Will recheck with each chemotherapy cycle. Expect continued decline toward normalization with effective chemotherapy. The absolute normalization milestone (<35 U/mL) is a meaningful indicator of treatment efficacy and will be tracked throughout the adjuvant course.

5. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY MONITORING:
   Baseline neurological exam today is normal. Patient has no pre-existing neuropathy. She will be monitored for cumulative PTX-related neuropathy throughout treatment. Dose reduction (to 135 mg/m2) or discontinuation of PTX may be required if significant neuropathy develops (Grade 2 or higher). Patient educated regarding symptoms to report.

6. GENETIC COUNSELING / HEREDITARY CANCER:
   Germline BRCA1 c.5266dupC (pathogenic) previously confirmed and communicated to patient. Genetic counseling referral placed. Patient has informed her adult children and other first-degree relatives. Cascade genetic testing for first-degree relatives is strongly recommended and was discussed in detail, with emphasis on her daughters' elevated lifetime risk for breast and ovarian cancer. Risk-reduction counseling for female relatives will be coordinated through the cancer genetics program.

7. SUPPORTIVE CARE / SURVIVORSHIP:
   Patient referred to oncology social work for psychosocial support and anxiety management around chemotherapy initiation. Nutrition consultation offered given post-surgical recovery. Will provide written patient education materials on chemotherapy side effect management. Discussed plans for return to work - targeted for after completion of chemotherapy in approximately 4-5 months.

FOLLOW-UP: Patient will return to the infusion suite for Cycle 1, Paraplatin/Taxol, in approximately 1 week. Next clinic visit in 3 weeks for Cycle 2 assessment.

Time spent: 55 minutes, >50% counseling and coordination of care.
